patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative but vague 7-year-old. Speak in short, simple sentences; let a parent fill details if prompted. tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies the primary condition so subsequent counseling and management align with evidence-based cluster headache care.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'cluster headache' (familial context may be mentioned but must not replace the diagnosis).

Demonstrates L1-appropriate diagnostic reasoning: unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain with ipsilateral autonomic features (e.g., tearing, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis) and/or restlessness, occurring in attacks with circadian/circannual clustering.

Screens for red flags and secondary causes that would change management (e.g., first/worst headache, new neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, age >50 new onset, cancer/immunosuppression, trauma, persistent atypical symptoms) and indicates need for urgent evaluation/imaging if present.

Differentiates from key mimics in explanation (at least migraine and trigeminal neuralgia or sinusitis), emphasizing short duration, autonomic signs, and agitation/restlessness typical of cluster headache.
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the term 'cluster headache'.

Communication milestone: explains first-line acute treatments and must include both 'oxygen' (high-flow) and 'sumatriptan' as standard abortive options (discussion-only is acceptable at L1; no prescribing tool call required).

Required safety checks before any prescribing: if the agent proposes prescribing any medication, they must perform and document (in dialogue) an allergy check, contraindication check (e.g., triptans contraindicated in ischemic heart disease/uncontrolled hypertension/stroke/hemiplegic or basilar migraine), and drug interaction check (e.g., serotonergic agents/MAO inhibitors with triptans). Failure on any is a critical error.

Avoids unsafe or misleading advice: does not recommend opioids as routine therapy; does not suggest OTC decongestants/antibiotics as primary treatment; does not advise delaying urgent care when red flags are present.

Provides basic anticipatory guidance: typical course with bouts, common triggers (alcohol during a bout), and advises follow-up for preventive therapy consideration (e.g., verapamil) without needing to prescribe at L1.
